lens.included_activities: Independent U.S. firms performing repeat outsourced production of engineered carbon, graphite, and metal-graphite products, including brushes, electrodes, fibers, and machined or formed components for external customers.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive plants, raw graphite mining, graphite gasket manufacturing, commodity resale, one-off development shops without repeat production, and branded product businesses that do not provide an outsourced production relationship.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat business-to-business programs based on drawings, specifications, qualification, machining or forming capability, and recurring purchase orders; customers include steelmakers, battery and energy users, motor and electrical OEMs, refractory users, and industrial equipment manufacturers.
lens.deviation_from_default: Narrowed to repeat outsourced engineered production because 335991 mixes electrodes, fibers, brushes, and other products, while much of the category is proprietary or commodity product manufacturing rather than the default outsourced-service model.

a.bridge: BLS occupation counts for the broader NAICS 335 subsector are combined with ILO task exposure by broad occupation to infer the wage-weighted AI-substitutable share in 335991. The bridge may overstate exposure if this niche is more production-heavy and understate it where engineering, quoting, and quality documentation are unusually intensive.
a.rationale: AI can address estimating, customer documentation, quality records, scheduling, procurement, and administrative work, but high-temperature processing, machining, inspection, and material handling remain physical.
a.change_evidence: A 335991 staffing matrix by occupation and wage, paired with task-level time studies and current automation inventories at representative plants, would materially change the estimate.
caveats: The occupation mix is for NAICS 335, not 335991.
ILO exposure measures technical potential rather than current unautomated opportunity.

rho.rationale: Digital quoting, planning, document search, inspection support, and workflow automation are implementable, but small-plant data quality, customer qualification, safety, and process variability slow deployment.
rho.change_evidence: Plant-level AI pilots reporting adoption, error rates, cycle time, headcount avoidance, integration cost, and customer acceptance over twelve months would materially change the range.
caveats: No five-year adoption study specific to carbon and graphite plants was found.
Implementation varies sharply with production digitization and product criticality.

e.rationale: Only firms acting as repeat outsourced engineered producers fit the service lens; vertically integrated electrode, fiber, and branded-product businesses do not. The product diversity and capital intensity further reduce eligibility within the estimated LMM universe.
e.change_evidence: A verified 335991 company roster classified by ownership, revenue model, EBITDA, customer recurrence, and captive status would materially change the estimate.
caveats: The frozen 57-firm estimate is not a verified company count.
NAICS assignment does not reveal whether revenue is outsourced, captive, proprietary, or recurring.

s5.bridge: The Census share of responding employer-business owners age 55 or older is discounted into a five-year qualifying-control-transfer probability for eligible 335991 firms. It may overstate transfers because age does not imply a sale, but may understate pressure in capital-intensive firms lacking family succession.
s5.rationale: Broad owner aging supports a succession pipeline, but no industry-specific owner-age or deal-flow series was found.
s5.change_evidence: Owner-age data for identified targets, broker mandates, family succession plans, and a five-year database of control transactions in engineered graphite components would materially change the estimate.
caveats: The Census statistic covers responding owners of employer businesses economy-wide.
Multiple owners can represent one firm and older ownership need not result in an external control transfer.

q.rationale: Quoted and fixed-price engineered programs can retain internal productivity gains between repricing cycles, while sophisticated industrial customers, competitive rebids, indexed materials, and cost-plus terms can force sharing.
q.change_evidence: Representative contracts, bid histories, price-adjustment clauses, renewal cadence, gross-margin bridges, and customer concentration by target would materially change the range.
caveats: No public billing-structure distribution exists for the screened lens.
Material-price changes can obscure retention of labor-driven gains.

d5.bridge: USGS graphite consumption and application data plus EIA evidence on electric-arc-furnace penetration are translated into constant-quality demand for outsourced carbon and graphite products. The bridge can overstate the lens if growth accrues to integrated battery-material producers and understate it if qualified domestic component capacity benefits from supply-chain localization.
d5.rationale: Battery, fuel-cell, electrical, refractory, and EAF uses create long-run support, but U.S. apparent natural-graphite consumption fell from 79,700 tons in 2022 to 52,000 in 2024 and the category spans mature products as well as growth markets.
d5.change_evidence: NAPCS product shipments, target backlogs, customer forecasts, battery and EAF capacity additions, and constant-price volume by product family would materially change the range.
caveats: Raw natural-graphite consumption is not equivalent to finished 335991 output.
Synthetic graphite and carbon-fiber dynamics differ from natural graphite.
End-market growth may be captured by large integrated producers outside the lens.

o.rationale: Customers still need accountable suppliers to qualify materials, operate furnaces and machining assets, inspect parts, manage traceability, and deliver physical products. Software may streamline interfaces but cannot replace the operator.
o.change_evidence: Evidence of customers insourcing qualified graphite production, additive or alternative-material substitution, lights-out production, or software-only procurement replacing supplier responsibility would materially change the estimate.
caveats: The estimate separates generative-AI displacement from conventional industrial automation.
Some low-complexity design and procurement work could become more self-served.

narrative.executive_view: The attractive part of 335991 is a narrow engineered-component and recurring-production subset, not the full product industry. Qualification and specialized process know-how can create durable relationships, but only a small share of the estimated firm universe likely fits the outsourced-service lens.
narrative.ai_impact: AI should improve quoting, planning, documentation, purchasing, quality analysis, and customer service; the physical and safety-critical production core remains labor- and equipment-dependent.
narrative.value_capture: Operators can retain value through faster quotes, lower administrative load, better yield analysis, and procurement scale, especially under fixed-price programs with slow repricing; competitive rebids and material-index clauses reduce retention.
narrative.firm_availability: The frozen dataset estimates 57 LMM-band firms, but product manufacturers, captive operations, and integrated suppliers substantially reduce the eligible pool. Succession is plausible but supported only by economy-wide owner-age evidence.
narrative.demand_durability: Energy storage, EAF steelmaking, electrical equipment, refractory, and advanced-material uses support demand, while recent natural-graphite consumption weakness and mature brush or electrode segments justify a wide range.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Key risks are an overly small eligible universe, product-family incompatibility, customer qualification barriers, energy and feedstock volatility, cyclicality in steel and industrial markets, and growth accruing to large integrated battery suppliers.
narrative.principal_driver: Qualified, repeat engineered production serving diversified electrical, steel, energy-storage, and industrial applications.
narrative.principal_weakness: Most of the NAICS category is product manufacturing rather than outsourced service, sharply limiting eligible acquisition supply.

sources.title: North American Industry Classification System: 335991 Carbon and Graphite Product Manufacturing
sources.supports: The six-digit industry boundary, included product families, and the exclusion of carbon or graphite gaskets.

sources.title: Electrical Equipment, Appliance, and Component Manufacturing: NAICS 335
sources.supports: Broader-sector occupation mix showing substantial assembly, supervision, and inspection employment, plus recent output context.

sources.title: Generative AI and Jobs: A global analysis of potential effects on job quantity and quality
sources.supports: Task-level GenAI exposure by broad occupation, with clerical work much more exposed than other occupational groups.

sources.title: Business Owners' Ages
sources.supports: Economy-wide evidence that 51% of responding employer-business owners were age 55 or older.

sources.title: Mineral Commodity Summaries 2025: Graphite (Natural)
sources.supports: U.S. graphite consumption, imports, major end uses, and recent consumption trend through 2024.

sources.title: U.S. coke production and consumption have declined more than 75% since 1980
sources.supports: The 2025 electric-arc-furnace share of U.S. steel production and continuing shift toward EAF steelmaking.

sources.title: Methodology and technical input for the 2025 U.S. List of Critical Minerals
sources.supports: Direct mapping of natural and synthetic graphite consumption and relevant NAPCS shipments to NAICS 335991.
